learning_outcomes.text: Classify macronutrients and micronutrients and identify common functions and food sources of each.
Describe the recommended levels of intake of macronutrients and micronutrients and how these change through the lifespan and during illness.
Explain how macronutrients and micronutrients are used in physiological processes.

policies.summary: This course syllabus is subject to modification by the instructor during the semester.
The grading scale is consistent with university policy: 100-90 = A, 89-80 = B, 79-70 = C, 69-60 = D, Below 60 = F.

Regular and punctual class attendance is essential. Students are responsible for missed material. Exceeding 4 allowable absences may result in an 'F' grade. Consistent late arrival or early departure may be recorded as an absence.
Late assignments incur a penalty of ten points per day. Missed assignments or exams receive a zero score.
Guidelines for online communication including writing in digestible chunks, avoiding all caps, careful use of emojis/humor, assuming best intentions, and respecting differences.
Students are expected to uphold intellectual and academic integrity, with definitions provided for plagiarism, cheating, unauthorized collaboration, falsification, and multiple submissions.
Students can withdraw from no more than 15 semester hours during their undergraduate career after the drop/add deadline.
Elizabeth City State University provides accommodations to students with documented disabilities; formal requests must be made through the Office of Student Accessibility & Testing Services.
The course is worth 3 credit hours, meaning students are expected to complete 9 hours of work per week in a normal 16-week semester (based on 3 hours work per 1 credit hour).

recommendations.title: Transparent AI Use in Course Design
recommendations.description: Instructors should clearly disclose in the syllabus (P1) if and how AI tools are used for course material generation or grading assistance to maintain student trust and clarity.
risks: Student confusion about course material origin
Perception of unfairness if AI assists grading without disclosure
Lack of understanding regarding AI's role
mitigations: Include an explicit AI disclosure statement in the syllabus (P1)
Discuss AI use during the first class session
Provide rationale for AI integration

recommendations.title: Promote Ethical Student AI Tool Use
recommendations.description: Provide explicit guidelines (aligned with P6) on acceptable and unacceptable uses of AI tools by students for assignments, research, and studying to prevent academic dishonesty.
risks: Plagiarism or unauthorized assistance (P6)
Erosion of learning and skill degradation
Misunderstanding of academic integrity expectations
mitigations: Update Academic Honesty Policy (P6) to specifically address AI tool usage
Provide clear examples of appropriate and inappropriate AI use
Design assignments that require critical thinking beyond AI capabilities

recommendations.title: Ensure Fairness and Equity in AI-Assisted Assessment
recommendations.description: If AI tools are used to assist with grading or feedback (A1, A2), faculty must critically review AI outputs for bias (P2) and ensure equitable application across all students.
risks: Algorithmic bias leading to unfair or inconsistent grades (P2)
Exacerbating existing educational inequalities
Providing inaccurate or unhelpful feedback
mitigations: Faculty should remain the final authority on grading (P2)
Conduct regular bias audits of AI tools used in assessment
Augment AI feedback with human review and personalized comments

recommendations.title: Protect Student Privacy with AI Tools
recommendations.description: Exercise caution when using AI tools that require student data input (e.g., ADA policy's confidentiality aspect P8), ensuring compliance with university data privacy policies and avoiding sharing sensitive student information with third-party AI.
risks: Unauthorized data access or breaches
Misuse of student data by third-party AI providers
Student discomfort or non-compliance with privacy regulations
mitigations: Prioritize university-approved AI tools
Minimize personal data input into AI systems
Inform students about data handling practices of AI tools (P8)
Review privacy policies of all AI tools used

recommendations.title: Develop AI Literacy and Critical Thinking Skills
recommendations.description: Integrate discussions or activities that help students understand how AI works, its limitations, potential biases, and ethical implications, aligning with the course's goal of expanding knowledge (e8043e6b8798).
risks: Students blindly trusting AI outputs without critical evaluation
Inability to discern misinformation generated by AI
Lack of preparedness for an AI-driven professional world
mitigations: Incorporate AI critique and evaluation into assignments
Facilitate class discussions on AI ethics and societal impact
Use AI as a learning tool to demonstrate its capabilities and flaws

recommendations.title: Faculty Professional Development in AI Integration
recommendations.description: Encourage and provide opportunities for faculty (e8043e6b8798) to receive training on the effective and responsible integration of AI into their teaching practices, ensuring pedagogical innovation and ethical use.
risks: Ineffective or irresponsible AI implementation due to lack of knowledge
Missed opportunities for pedagogical enhancement
Increased workload for faculty without adequate support
mitigations: Offer university-sponsored workshops and training sessions
Facilitate peer learning and sharing of best practices
Provide access to curated resources on AI in education

recommendations.title: Adapt Assessment Design for an AI-Enabled World
recommendations.description: Modify assessment (A1, A2) strategies to emphasize higher-order thinking, creativity, problem-solving, and application of knowledge that AI tools currently struggle to fully replicate, thereby reinforcing true learning outcomes (LO1, LO2, LO3).
risks: AI-generated submissions masking true student understanding
Decreased academic rigor if assessments are easily solvable by AI
Undermining the value of learning outcomes
mitigations: Design process-based assignments requiring iterative steps and reflection
Incorporate oral presentations, debates, or real-world application projects
Utilize in-class, supervised, or personalized assessments (A1, A2)
